content:
# Leviticus 11 — Disagreement Log

**Chapter:** Leviticus 11 (47 verses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Agents compared:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic)
**Total disagreements logged:** 58

---

## Format

Each entry lists:
- **Verse** and the Hebrew term or phrase at issue
- **EMET** rendering
- **LOGOS** rendering
- **PNEUMA** rendering
- **SOPHIA ruling** (consensus choice)
- **Rationale**

---

## Disagreements by Verse

---

### V. 1 — Name forms for Moses and Aaron

**Hebrew:** Moshe / Aharon

- **EMET:** "Moshe" / "Aharon" (Hebrew forms)
- **LOGOS:** "Moses" / "Aaron" (English forms)
- **PNEUMA:** "Moses" / "Aaron" (English forms)
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Moses" / "Aaron"
- **Rationale:** Project convention uses English name forms throughout. EMET's use of Hebrew name-forms here is inconsistent with established convention. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree; consensus follows.

---

### V. 2 — "sons of Israel" vs "people of Israel"

**Hebrew:** bney yisrael (בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל)

- **EMET:** "sons of Yisra'el" (Hebrew name-form)
- **LOGOS:** "sons of Israel"
- **PNEUMA:** "people of Israel"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "sons of Israel"
- **Rationale:** "Sons of Israel" is the established project-wide convention for bney yisrael. PNEUMA's "people of Israel" is interpretively accurate but departs from the formal convention. EMET's "Yisra'el" is rejected for the same name-form reason as v. 1.

---

### V. 2 — "living creatures" vs "animals" — the ha-chayyah/behemah distinction

**Hebrew:** ha-chayyah (הַחַיָּה) … min-kol-ha-behemah (מִן-כָּל-הַבְּהֵמָה)

- **EMET:** "living-creatures … from among all the animals"
- **LOGOS:** "living creatures … from among all the animals"
- **PNEUMA:** "living creatures … from among all the animals on the land" (adds "on the land" as the rendering of asher al-ha-arets)
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "living creatures … from among all the animals that are on the land"
- **Rationale:** All three substantially agree. PNEUMA relocates "on the land" to the end of the NP rather than making it a separate clause — the consensus uses LOGOS's clause structure with PNEUMA's inclusion of the qualifier at the end.

---

### V. 3 — The dual-criterion formula for clean land animals

**Hebrew:** mafriset parsah ve-shosa'at shesa' parsa'ot ma'alat gerah (מַפְרֶסֶת פַּרְסָה וְשֹׁסַעַת שֶׁסַע פְּרָסֹת מַעֲלַת גֵּרָה)

- **EMET:** "Every a divider-of-the-hoof and a shearer-of-the-sheaf and that brings up the cud — among the animals — it [you may eat]"
- **LOGOS:** "Whatever parts the hoof and is cloven-footed and chews the cud, among the animals, that you may eat."
- **PNEUMA:** "Any animal that has split hooves — hooves divided in two — and that chews its cud: you may eat it."
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Every animal that parts the hoof and is cloven-footed and chews the cud — that you may eat."
- **Rationale:** EMET's "divider-of-the-hoof" and "shearer-of-the-sheaf" are excessively hyphenated and opaque; LOGOS's three-part formula is the clearest formal rendering of all three Hebrew criteria; PNEUMA's "split hooves — hooves divided in two" is an interpretive gloss that adds a parenthetical not in the Hebrew at this position. The consensus adopts LOGOS's formula with a simplified opening ("Every animal that").

---

### V. 4 — Opening adversative connector

**Hebrew:** akh (אַךְ) — "nevertheless/but/however"

- **EMET:** "Nevertheless —"
- **LOGOS:** "Nevertheless, among those that chew the cud or part the hoof, you shall not eat these:"
- **PNEUMA:** "But you must not eat the following animals that only chew their cud or only have split hooves:"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Nevertheless, from among those that chew the cud or that part the hoof, you shall not eat these:"
- **Rationale:** EMET's bare "Nevertheless" with em-dash is too abrupt. PNEUMA's "only … or only" adds interpretive precision not in the Hebrew. LOGOS's fuller clause is adopted with "from among" added to match EMET's faithful rendering of the Hebrew preposition min.

---

### V. 4 — "is unclean to you" vs "is unclean for you"

**Hebrew:** tameh hu' la-khem (טָמֵא הוּא לָכֶם)

- **EMET:** "it is unclean for you"
- **LOGOS:** "is unclean to you"
- **PNEUMA:** "it is unclean for you"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "it is unclean to you"
- **Rationale:** Two-to-one for "for you" (EMET, PNEUMA), but LOGOS's "to you" better captures the dative of disadvantage in la-khem and aligns with the majority of formal translations (ESV, NASB). The consensus adopts "unclean to you" as the consistent rendering throughout the chapter (vv. 4–8, 26–28, etc.).

---

### V. 7 — Pig's hoof description

**Hebrew:** mafris parsah ve-shosa' shesa' (מַפְרִיס פַּרְסָה וְשֹׁסַע שֶׁסַע)

- **EMET:** "for it a divider of the hoof and shearer of the cleft but the cud it does not bring up"
- **LOGOS:** "because it parts the hoof and is cloven-footed but does not chew the cud"
- **PNEUMA:** "it does have split hooves, hooves divided in two, but it does not chew the cud"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "for it parts the hoof and is cloven-footed but does not chew the cud"
- **Rationale:** Follows LOGOS's standard formula, parallel with v. 3. PNEUMA's double gloss ("split hooves, hooves divided in two") is interpretive. EMET's "shearer of the cleft" is opaque.

---

### V. 8 — "carcass" handling prohibition

**Hebrew:** u-venivlatam lo' tiggau (וּבְנִבְלָתָם לֹא תִגָּעוּ)

- **EMET:** "and their carcass you shall not touch"
- **LOGOS:** "and their carcasses you shall not touch"
- **PNEUMA:** "and you must not touch their carcasses"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "and their carcasses you shall not touch"
- **Rationale:** Word-order difference (EMET/LOGOS follow Hebrew VSO; PNEUMA inverts). Plural "carcasses" preferred over EMET's singular (nevelatam is a collective plural). LOGOS's rendering is adopted.

---

### V. 9 — "of all that are in the waters" — structural difference

**Hebrew:** min-kol asher ba-mayim (מִן-כֹּל אֲשֶׁר בַּמַּיִם)

- **EMET:** "These from all that is in the waters: every fins and scales…"
- **LOGOS:** "These you may eat of all that are in the waters: everything in the waters that has fins and scales…"
- **PNEUMA:** "Of all the creatures living in the water — in the seas or the rivers — you may eat any creature that has fins and scales."
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "These you may eat of all that are in the waters: everything in the waters that has fins and scales — whether in the seas or in the rivers — that you may eat."
- **Rationale:** EMET's "every fins" is a grammatical error (Hebrew kol here is adjectival). LOGOS accurately renders the structure; PNEUMA adds "creatures living in" where the Hebrew simply says "in." The consensus adopts LOGOS, relocating the "seas or rivers" qualifier after "fins and scales" to match Hebrew word order.

---

### V. 10 — "swarming creatures of the water" vs "swarming things of the water"

**Hebrew:** sherets ha-mayim (שֶׁרֶץ הַמַּיִם)

- **EMET:** "swarmers of the water"
- **LOGOS:** "swarming creatures of the water"
- **PNEUMA:** "swarming things of the water"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "swarming creatures of the water"
- **Rationale:** "Swarming creatures" (LOGOS) is preferred over "swarmers" (neologism, EMET) and "swarming things" (PNEUMA — too vague, loses the animate sense of sherets). LOGOS adopted.

---

### V. 10 — "of all the living things that are in the water" — phrase inclusion

**Hebrew:** u-mikol nefesh ha-chayyah asher ba-mayim (וּמִכֹּל נֶפֶשׁ הַחַיָּה אֲשֶׁר בַּמַּיִם)

- **EMET:** "and from all the living-things that are in the water"
- **LOGOS:** "and of all the living creatures that are in the water"
- **PNEUMA:** "among all the living things in the water"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "and of all the living things that are in the water"
- **Rationale:** "Living things" preferred over "living creatures" here to avoid repetition with the "creatures" used for sherets just before, and to preserve nefesh chayyah's broader scope. EMET and PNEUMA agree on "things"; consensus adopts "of all the living things."

---

### V. 11 — "their carcasses you shall regard as detestable"

**Hebrew:** u-nevelatam teshaqtsevu (וְאֶת-נִבְלָתָם תְּשַׁקֵּצוּ)

- **EMET:** "their carcasses you shall treat-as-detestable"
- **LOGOS:** "their carcasses you shall regard as detestable"
- **PNEUMA:** "you must regard their carcasses as detestable"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "their carcasses you shall regard as detestable"
- **Rationale:** "Regard as detestable" (LOGOS) is preferred over EMET's hyphenated compound and PNEUMA's modal "must." Word-order follows Hebrew (object before verb): "their carcasses you shall regard as detestable."

---

### V. 13 — Opening formula for the bird list

**Hebrew:** ve-et-elleh teshaqtsevu min-ha-'of (וְאֶת-אֵלֶּה תְּשַׁקְּצוּ מִן-הָעוֹף)

- **EMET:** "And these that you shall treat-as-detestable from the birds"
- **LOGOS:** "And these you shall regard as detestable among the birds"
- **PNEUMA:** "Of birds, these are the ones you are to regard as detestable"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "And these you shall regard as detestable among the birds"
- **Rationale:** LOGOS's rendering is the most balanced. PNEUMA's restructuring loses the Hebrew's fronted "and these." EMET's "treat-as-detestable" is awkward. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### V. 13 — peres: "bearded vulture" vs "vulture"

**Hebrew:** peres (פֶּרֶס)

- **EMET:** "the bearded-vulture"
- **LOGOS:** "the bearded vulture"
- **PNEUMA:** "the vulture" (no species specification)
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the bearded vulture"
- **Rationale:** PNEUMA's generic "vulture" is not specific enough for a text that carefully lists distinct species. Modern ornithological consensus identifies peres as the bearded vulture (Gypaetus barbatus). EMET and LOGOS agree; the consensus follows.

---

### V. 13 — 'ozniyyah: "black vulture" vs unspecified

**Hebrew:** 'ozniyyah (עָזְנִיָּה)

- **EMET:** "and the black-vulture"
- **LOGOS:** "the black vulture"
- **PNEUMA:** "the black vulture"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the black vulture"
- **Rationale:** All three agree on species; only punctuation/conjunction differs. Consensus adopts LOGOS's form.

---

### V. 14 — da'ah / raah: "kite" — agreement

**Hebrew:** da'ah (דָּאָה) / raah (רָאָה in Deut 14:13)

- **EMET:** "the kite"
- **LOGOS:** "the kite"
- **PNEUMA:** "the red kite"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the kite"
- **Rationale:** PNEUMA's "red kite" specifies Milvus milvus in particular; the Hebrew allows for either species of kite (red or black). The more general "the kite" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred to avoid over-specification.

---

### V. 14 — ayyah: "falcon of any kind" vs "black kites of every kind"

**Hebrew:** ve-et-ha-ayyah le-mineha (וְאֶת-הָאַיָּה לְמִינָהּ)

- **EMET:** "and the falcon after its kind"
- **LOGOS:** "the falcon of any kind"
- **PNEUMA:** "black kites of every kind"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the falcon of any kind"
- **Rationale:** PNEUMA conflates da'ah and ayyah under the "kite" identification — rendering two distinct species as subspecies of the same bird. Modern scholarship (DCH, HALOT, Milgrom) consistently distinguishes ayyah ("falcon" or "kite-hawk") from da'ah ("kite"). EMET and LOGOS agree on "falcon"; the consensus follows.

---

### V. 16 — tachmas: "nighthawk" vs "screech owl" vs "horned owl"

**Hebrew:** tachmass (תַּחְמָס)

- **EMET:** "and the nighthawk"
- **LOGOS:** "the nighthawk"
- **PNEUMA:** "the screech owl" / "the horned owl" (note: PNEUMA inconsistently uses "horned owl" in the text and discusses "screech owl" in the notes)
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the nighthawk"
- **Rationale:** EMET and LOGOS agree on "nighthawk." PNEUMA's "horned owl" / "screech owl" introduces a different bird family altogether. The LXX renders tachmas as γλαῦκα ("owl") but the Syriac and Targum support a nocturnal flier rather than an owl specifically. "Nighthawk" (a nightjar-type bird) is preferred for the nighttime, swooping flight implied by the Hebrew root. LOGOS/EMET adopted.

---

### V. 17 — yanshuf: "great owl" vs "short-eared owl"

**Hebrew:** yanshuf (יַנְשׁוּף)

- **EMET:** "and the great-owl"
- **LOGOS:** "the short-eared owl"
- **PNEUMA:** "the great owl"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the great owl"
- **Rationale:** Two-to-one for "great owl" (EMET, PNEUMA). LOGOS's "short-eared owl" is one specific ornithological proposal among several. "Great owl" is a more defensible generic rendering for a term whose precise identification is uncertain, and it matches the LXX's ἶβις tradition of size-emphasis (though the LXX diverges entirely here). EMET/PNEUMA adopted.

---

### V. 18 — tinshmet (bird form): "barn owl" vs "white owl"

**Hebrew:** tinshemet (תִּנְשֶׁמֶת) in its bird sense

- **EMET:** "and the white-owl"
- **LOGOS:** "the barn owl"
- **PNEUMA:** "the white owl"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the barn owl"
- **Rationale:** "Barn owl" (Tyto alba) is the most widely supported identification among modern lexicographers (HALOT, DCH, Milgrom). The barn owl is common in Israel and is a distinctive owl clearly distinguishable from yanshuf ("great owl") named in the preceding verse. LOGOS adopted over EMET/PNEUMA's "white owl."

---

### V. 18 — qa'at: "desert owl" vs "pelican" vs "tawny owl"

**Hebrew:** qa'at (קָאַת)

- **EMET:** "and the pelican"
- **LOGOS:** "the tawny owl"
- **PNEUMA:** "the desert owl"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the desert owl"
- **Rationale:** All three agents disagree. EMET's "pelican" follows LXX πελεκᾶνα but is implausible in a list of raptors and owls (pelicans are not found in desert regions and are not birds of prey). LOGOS's "tawny owl" is an owl species proposal. PNEUMA's "desert owl" is a broader but contextually defensible rendering — a desert-dwelling owl like the eagle owl (Bubo bubo) fits the ecological context of the bird list. The consensus adopts "desert owl" as the most defensible mediating choice.

---

### V. 18 — racham: "carrion vulture" vs "vulture" vs "osprey"

**Hebrew:** racham (רָחָם)

- **EMET:** "and the vulture"
- **LOGOS:** "the carrion vulture"
- **PNEUMA:** "the osprey"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the carrion vulture"
- **Rationale:** All three disagree. PNEUMA's "osprey" follows a minority identification tradition. EMET's "vulture" is too generic. LOGOS's "carrion vulture" (Egyptian vulture, Neophron percnopterus) is the strongest identification — the Egyptian vulture is common in Israel, is a scavenger (matching racham's apparent root meaning), and is distinct from the bearded vulture (peres) and black vulture ('ozniyyah) already listed in v. 13. LOGOS adopted.

---

### V. 19 — chasidah: "stork" — agreement with note

**Hebrew:** chasidah (חֲסִידָה)

- **EMET:** "And the stork"
- **LOGOS:** "the stork"
- **PNEUMA:** "the stork"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the stork"
- **Rationale:** All three agree. Logged for completeness since the LXX diverges here (ἐρωδιόν = "heron"). The consensus follows the MT identification.

---

### V. 19 — anafah: "heron of any kind" vs "herons of every kind"

**Hebrew:** anafah le-mineha (אֲנָפָה לְמִינָהּ)

- **EMET:** "the heron after its kind"
- **LOGOS:** "the heron of any kind"
- **PNEUMA:** "herons of every kind"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the heron of any kind"
- **Rationale:** All three agree on the bird; the variation is "of any kind" vs "after its kind" vs "of every kind." The consensus uses "of any kind" (LOGOS) as the most natural English rendering of le-mineha in this chapter's repeated formula (cf. vv. 14, 15, 16, 22).

---

### V. 20 — "all fours" vs "four legs"

**Hebrew:** ha-holekh al-arba' (הַהֹלֵךְ עַל-אַרְבַּע)

- **EMET:** "that walks on four"
- **LOGOS:** "that go on all fours"
- **PNEUMA:** "that walk on all fours"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "that go on all fours"
- **Rationale:** EMET's "walks on four" is a bare literal rendering. LOGOS and PNEUMA both use the idiomatic "go on all fours" / "walk on all fours." The consensus prefers the idiomatic form; "go" (LOGOS) is slightly preferred over "walk" (PNEUMA) as the Hebrew holekh ("walker") is more general than a specifically walking motion.

---

### V. 21 — "jointed legs" vs "knees" — the kherayim rendering

**Hebrew:** kherayim (כְרָעַיִם) — the jumping-leg apparatus of locusts

- **EMET:** "which has knees above its legs"
- **LOGOS:** "that have jointed legs above their feet, with which to hop on the ground"
- **PNEUMA:** "those that have jointed legs above their feet for leaping on the ground"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "those that have jointed legs above their feet, with which to leap on the ground"
- **Rationale:** EMET's "knees" is a defensible literal rendering of kherayim (from the root for thigh/knee joint) but does not communicate the functional anatomical description. LOGOS's "jointed legs" is the standard formal rendering. The consensus adopts LOGOS's "jointed legs above their feet" with PNEUMA's "to leap" over LOGOS's "to hop" (the locust's motion is more accurately described as a leap than a hop).

---

### V. 22 — Insect species: "bald locust" rendering

**Hebrew:** sol'am (סָלְעָם) — second of the four permitted insects

- **EMET:** "the locust-variant le-mino"
- **LOGOS:** "the bald locust of any kind"
- **PNEUMA:** "the bald locust of every kind"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the bald locust of any kind"
- **Rationale:** EMET preserves the Hebrew term le-mino without translating (a departure from the project norm of English rendering). LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on "bald locust"; the consensus adopts LOGOS.

---

### V. 22 — Insect species: "cricket" rendering

**Hebrew:** chargol (חַרְגֹּל) — third of the four permitted insects

- **EMET:** "and the cricket le-mino"
- **LOGOS:** "the cricket of any kind"
- **PNEUMA:** "the cricket of every kind"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the cricket of any kind"
- **Rationale:** All three agree on "cricket"; LOGOS's "of any kind" is adopted as the chapter's consistent formula rendering.

---

### V. 24 — "by these you shall become unclean" vs "and by these"

**Hebrew:** u-le-elleh tittam'u (וּלְאֵלֶּה תִּטַּמָּאוּ)

- **EMET:** "And by these you will be made-unclean"
- **LOGOS:** "And by these you shall become unclean."
- **PNEUMA:** "By the following creatures you will become unclean"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "And by these you shall become unclean."
- **Rationale:** PNEUMA's "the following creatures" is interpretive (the Hebrew simply says "by these"). EMET's passive "be made-unclean" and LOGOS's middle "become unclean" reflect two different understandings of the nifal (which can be both passive and reflexive); "become unclean" (LOGOS) is preferred as it captures the reflexive/resultative force of the nifal. PNEUMA drops the "and" (ve-) at verse opening; the consensus preserves it.

---

### V. 25 — "carries any part of" vs "carries from" vs "picks up"

**Hebrew:** ve-ha-nose' mi-nivelatam (וְהַנֹּשֵׂא מִנִּבְלָתָם) — "and the one who carries from their carcass"

- **EMET:** "And everyone who carries from their carcass"
- **LOGOS:** "and whoever carries any part of their carcass"
- **PNEUMA:** "and anyone who picks up their carcass"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "and whoever carries any part of their carcass"
- **Rationale:** EMET's "carries from" is literal but awkward. LOGOS's "any part of" explicates the partitive min— correctly. PNEUMA's "picks up" loses the sense of carrying (nasa' = to lift and carry, not merely to pick up). LOGOS adopted.

---

### V. 26 — "is not cloven-footed" — structural rendering

**Hebrew:** ve-shesa' enannu shasua' (וְשֶׁסַע אֵינֶנּוּ שָׁסוּעַ) — "and the cleft is not split"

- **EMET:** "but is not cleaving-a-cleft"
- **LOGOS:** "but is not cloven-footed"
- **PNEUMA:** "is not fully split"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "but is not cloven-footed"
- **Rationale:** EMET's "cleaving-a-cleft" is opaque. PNEUMA's "fully split" is interpretive (the Hebrew does not add "fully"; the point is the complete absence of the split). LOGOS's "cloven-footed" is the standard English term for this criterion and was established in vv. 3 and 7. LOGOS adopted for consistency.

---

### V. 27 — "walk on their paws" — agreement

**Hebrew:** kol holekh al-kappav (כֹּל הֹלֵךְ עַל-כַּפָּיו) — "everything that walks on its palms/paws"

- **EMET:** "everything that walks on its paws"
- **LOGOS:** "all that walk on their paws"
- **PNEUMA:** "Every animal that walks on its paws"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "all that walk on their paws"
- **Rationale:** All three agree on "paws" (kappav = palms, used for paws of carnivores). The consensus adopts LOGOS's "all that walk on their paws" as the most inclusive rendering of the plural subject.

---

### V. 29 — "mole rat" vs "mole"

**Hebrew:** choled (חֹלֶד)

- **EMET:** "the mole"
- **LOGOS:** "the mole rat"
- **PNEUMA:** "the mole rat"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the mole rat"
- **Rationale:** The European mole (Talpa europaea) is not native to the Levant; the mole rat (Spalax spp.) is common in Israel and is the more likely identification for choled. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree; the consensus follows.

---

### V. 29 — "great lizard of any kind" vs "great lizard of every kind"

**Hebrew:** ha-tsav le-minehu (הַצָּב לְמִינֵהוּ)

- **EMET:** "and the great-lizard after its kind"
- **LOGOS:** "and the great lizard of any kind"
- **PNEUMA:** "the great lizard of every kind"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the great lizard of any kind"
- **Rationale:** See v. 22 rationale: "of any kind" is the consistent chapter formula. LOGOS adopted.

---

### V. 30 — letaah (fourth reptile): "lizard" vs "skink" vs "sand lizard"

**Hebrew:** letaah (לְטָאָה)

- **EMET:** "and the gecko-lizard"
- **LOGOS:** "the lizard"
- **PNEUMA:** "the common lizard"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the lizard"
- **Rationale:** The Hebrew letaah is a broad term for a lizard type; over-specification (EMET's "gecko-lizard," PNEUMA's "common lizard") imports more certainty than the evidence supports. LOGOS's simple "the lizard" is adopted.

---

### V. 30 — fifth reptile identification

**Hebrew:** chomet (חֹמֶט) — possibly "skink" or "sand lizard"

- **EMET:** "and the skink"
- **LOGOS:** "the sand lizard"
- **PNEUMA:** "the skink"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "the skink"
- **Rationale:** Two-to-one for "skink" (EMET, PNEUMA). The skink (family Scincidae) is well-attested in the Levant and is a plausible identification for chomet. LOGOS's "sand lizard" is also defensible. The consensus follows the two-agent majority: "the skink."

---

### V. 31 — "when they are dead" — agreement

**Hebrew:** be-motam (בְּמֹתָם)

- **EMET:** "at their death"
- **LOGOS:** "when they are dead"
- **PNEUMA:** "when they are dead"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "when they are dead"
- **Rationale:** EMET's "at their death" is a literal rendering of the Hebrew prepositional phrase but sounds more like a death event than a state. "When they are dead" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) captures the state of being dead at the moment of contact. Two-to-one; consensus follows.

---

### V. 32 — "a garment" vs "a piece of clothing" vs "garment-cloth"

**Hebrew:** beged (בֶּגֶד) — garment, cloth, article of clothing

- **EMET:** "garment-cloth"
- **LOGOS:** "a garment"
- **PNEUMA:** "a piece of clothing"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "a garment"
- **Rationale:** EMET's "garment-cloth" is a non-standard compound. PNEUMA's "a piece of clothing" is slightly paraphrastic. LOGOS's "a garment" is the standard English rendering of beged. LOGOS adopted.

---

### V. 32 — "skin" vs "leather item"

**Hebrew:** or (עוֹר) — skin, hide, leather

- **EMET:** "or skin"
- **LOGOS:** "or a skin"
- **PNEUMA:** "a leather item"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "or a skin"
- **Rationale:** EMET and LOGOS agree on "skin." PNEUMA's "leather item" is an interpretive expansion. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

### V. 32 — "any article that is used for any purpose" — explicitness

**Hebrew:** asher ye'aseh melacha bahem (אֲשֶׁר-יֵעָשֶׂה מְלָאכָה בָּהֶם) — "which is used for any work in them"

- **EMET:** "any vessel by which work is done"
- **LOGOS:** "any article that is used for any purpose"
- **PNEUMA:** "any other utensil used for work"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "any article that is used for any purpose"
- **Rationale:** LOGOS's phrasing is the clearest English rendering. EMET's "by which work is done" is slightly archaic. PNEUMA's "other utensil" introduces "other" not in the Hebrew. LOGOS adopted.

---

### V. 33 — "everything in it shall be unclean" — agreement

**Hebrew:** kol asher be-tokho yitma' (כֹּל אֲשֶׁר בְּתוֹכוֹ יִטְמָא)

- **EMET:** "everything in its interior shall be unclean"
- **LOGOS:** "all that is in it shall be unclean"
- **PNEUMA:** "everything in it becomes unclean"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "everything in it shall be unclean"
- **Rationale:** EMET's "interior" is a slight over-translation of be-tokho ("in it / inside it"). PNEUMA's "becomes" (present tense) shifts from the Hebrew's imperfect (future/modal). LOGOS's "all that is in it shall be unclean" is the most balanced, but "everything" (EMET/PNEUMA) is preferred for the universal force of kol asher. The consensus: "everything in it shall be unclean."

---

### V. 34 — "could be eaten" / "could be drunk" — modal force

**Hebrew:** asher ye'akhel (אֲשֶׁר יֵאָכֵל) — "that may be eaten" / asher yishshateh (אֲשֶׁר יִשָּׁתֶה) — "that may be drunk"

- **EMET:** "which may be eaten … which may be drunk"
- **LOGOS:** "that could be eaten … that could be drunk"
- **PNEUMA:** implied (PNEUMA: "any food that could be eaten … any drinkable liquid")
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "that could be eaten … that could be drunk"
- **Rationale:** LOGOS's "could be eaten/drunk" captures the nifal imperfect's potential-modal force more naturally than EMET's "may be." PNEUMA's "drinkable liquid" is an interpretation. LOGOS adopted.

---

### V. 35 — "cooking stand" vs "stove" vs "cooking-range"

**Hebrew:** kirayim (כִּירַיִם) — a double cooking stove or brazier

- **EMET:** "cooking-range"
- **LOGOS:** "stove"
- **PNEUMA:** "a cooking stand"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "cooking stand"
- **Rationale:** LOGOS's "stove" is too modern in connotation. EMET's "cooking-range" similarly evokes modern appliances. PNEUMA's "cooking stand" (a portable brazier/tripod) better reflects the ancient implement. PNEUMA adopted.

---

### V. 36 — "a gathering of water" (mikveh-mayim) — explicit vs implicit

**Hebrew:** mikveh mayim (מִקְוֵה מַיִם) — "a gathering/collection of waters" — the technical term for a ritual pool

- **EMET:** "an assembly of water"
- **LOGOS:** "holding water" (renders mikveh as an implied participle: "a cistern holding water")
- **PNEUMA:** "holding water" (same as LOGOS)
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "a gathering of water"
- **Rationale:** LOGOS and PNEUMA both absorb mikveh-mayim into the preceding noun as a participle modifier, eliding the distinct technical term. EMET preserves it as a separate noun phrase but uses "assembly," which is slightly abstract. The consensus renders mikveh explicitly as "a gathering of water" — this preserves the halakhic significance of the term (the mikveh as a ritual body of collected water) and is more accurate than treating it as a mere modifier.

---

### V. 37 — "seed grain that is to be sown"

**Hebrew:** zera' zeru'a (זֶרַע זֵרוּעַ) — "seed of sowing / seed for sowing"

- **EMET:** "seed-grain which is to be sown"
- **LOGOS:** "seed grain that is to be sown"
- **PNEUMA:** "seed grain that is to be planted"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "seed grain that is to be sown"
- **Rationale:** "Sown" (EMET/LOGOS) more precisely renders zeru'a (from zara', "to sow") than PNEUMA's "planted." Agricultural precision matters here since the verse turns on the condition of the seed. LOGOS adopted.

---

### V. 39 — "any animal that you may eat" vs "an animal that you are permitted to eat"

**Hebrew:** min-ha-behemah asher hi' lakhem le-okhlah (מִן-הַבְּהֵמָה אֲשֶׁר הִיא לָכֶם לְאָכְלָה) — "from the animals which are for you for eating"

- **EMET:** "any of the animals that is clean for you — dies"
- **LOGOS:** "any animal that you may eat dies"
- **PNEUMA:** "an animal that you are permitted to eat dies on its own"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "any animal that you may eat dies"
- **Rationale:** EMET adds "clean" which is interpretive (the Hebrew says "for eating," not "clean"). PNEUMA's "on its own" renders the implicit sense of nevelah (dying of itself) but the Hebrew here simply says "dies" (tamut). LOGOS's rendering is most faithful; the consensus follows.

---

### V. 40 — "carries its carcass" — gender of pronoun

**Hebrew:** ve-ha-nose' et-nivlatah (וְהַנֹּשֵׂא אֶת-נִבְלָתָהּ)

- **EMET:** "and he who carries its carcass"
- **LOGOS:** "And whoever carries the carcass"
- **PNEUMA:** "Anyone who picks up its carcass"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "And whoever carries its carcass"
- **Rationale:** LOGOS drops the possessive pronoun "its" which the Hebrew has (nivlatah = "its carcass," referring to the specific animal). PNEUMA uses "picks up" (see v. 25 note — nasa' = to carry, not merely pick up). The consensus restores "its" and uses "carries." EMET's "he who" is overly masculine-specific in a gender-inclusive context; "whoever" is preferred.

---

### V. 41 — "swarming creature" vs "swarming thing"

**Hebrew:** kol ha-sherets ha-shoretts al-ha-arets (כֹּל הַשֶּׁרֶץ הַשֹּׁרֵץ עַל-הָאָרֶץ)

- **EMET:** "every swarming-thing that swarms upon the earth"
- **LOGOS:** "Every swarming creature that swarms on the ground"
- **PNEUMA:** "Every creature that swarms on the ground"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Every swarming creature that swarms on the ground"
- **Rationale:** EMET's "swarming-thing" (see v. 10 note). LOGOS retains the polyptoton (sherets / shoretts = "swarming creature … that swarms"), which is present in the Hebrew as a rhetorical intensification. PNEUMA drops "swarming" from the subject, losing the figure. LOGOS adopted.

---

### V. 42 — "whatever goes on its belly" — serpent allusion

**Hebrew:** kol holekh al-gachon (כֹּל הֹלֵךְ עַל-גָּחוֹן) — "everything that goes on its belly"

- **EMET:** "Everything going on its belly"
- **LOGOS:** "Whatever goes on its belly"
- **PNEUMA:** "Whether it moves on its belly"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Whatever goes on its belly"
- **Rationale:** PNEUMA shifts from assertion to conditional ("whether it moves"), which alters the grammatical structure. EMET and LOGOS use declarative forms. LOGOS's "Whatever" is the most natural English rendering of kol. The serpent allusion to Gen 3:14 is present in all three notes but not in the translation text itself. LOGOS adopted.

---

### V. 42 — "or whatever has many legs" — the myriapod category

**Hebrew:** ve-kol marbe raglayim (וְכֹל מַרְבֵּה רַגְלַיִם) — "and everything multiplying legs"

- **EMET:** "to everything multiplying legs"
- **LOGOS:** "or whatever has many feet" (LOGOS uses "feet" instead of "legs")
- **PNEUMA:** "or has many legs"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "or whatever has many legs"
- **Rationale:** EMET's "multiplying legs" is too literal. LOGOS uses "feet" (raglayim can mean either feet or legs); PNEUMA uses "legs." In context, this phrase covers myriapods (centipedes/millipedes), where "legs" is the more natural English term. PNEUMA adopted.

---

### V. 43 — "so that you become unclean through them" — purpose/result

**Hebrew:** ve-nitmetem bam (וְנִטְמֵתֶם בָּם) — "and you will/shall become unclean by them"

- **EMET:** "and be not made unclean through them" (EMET appears to render this as a third prohibition rather than a result clause)
- **LOGOS:** "and become unclean through them"
- **PNEUMA:** "so that you become unclean through them"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "so that you become unclean through them"
- **Rationale:** The Hebrew ve-nitmetem bam is a consecutive imperfect expressing result/consequence of the preceding prohibitions. EMET incorrectly renders it as another prohibition. PNEUMA's "so that" makes the consequence structure explicit. The consensus follows PNEUMA.

---

### V. 44 — "Consecrate yourselves therefore" — the vav-consecutive connector

**Hebrew:** vehiqqadishtem (וְהִתְקַדִּשְׁתֶּם) — "and you shall consecrate yourselves"

- **EMET:** "and you shall consecrate yourselves"
- **LOGOS:** "Consecrate yourselves therefore"
- **PNEUMA:** "Consecrate yourselves, therefore"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Consecrate yourselves therefore"
- **Rationale:** EMET's bare "and" loses the logical connector "therefore" which both LOGOS and PNEUMA supply (it is implied by the preceding "for I am the LORD your God" — the consecration is the consequence). LOGOS/PNEUMA agreed; consensus follows.

---

### V. 44 — "for I am holy" — the imitatio Dei formula

**Hebrew:** ki qadosh ani (כִּי קָדוֹשׁ אָנִי)

- **EMET:** "for I holy"
- **LOGOS:** "for I am holy"
- **PNEUMA:** "because I am holy"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "for I am holy"
- **Rationale:** EMET drops the copula "am" (the Hebrew has no explicit verb here, but the copula is required in English). LOGOS and PNEUMA both supply "am." LOGOS's "for" (ki = causal) is preferred over PNEUMA's "because" as more concise in a formula that recurs twice (vv. 44–45) and that has a quasi-creedal rhythm in the tradition. LOGOS adopted.

---

### V. 44 — "You shall not defile yourselves with any swarming creature that swarms on the ground" — inclusion/omission

**Hebrew:** ve-lo' titam'u be-kol-ha-sherets ha-romes al-ha-arets (וְלֹא תְטַמְּאוּ בְּכָל-הַשֶּׁרֶץ הָרֹמֵשׂ עַל-הָאָרֶץ)

- **EMET:** "and do not make yourselves unclean with every swarming-thing that crawls on the earth"
- **LOGOS:** "You shall not defile yourselves with any swarming creature that crawls on the ground."
- **PNEUMA:** "You must not defile yourselves with any of the swarming creatures that swarm on the ground."
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "You shall not defile yourselves with any swarming creature that swarms on the ground."
- **Rationale:** EMET's "make yourselves unclean" is an acceptable rendering but "defile" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is slightly stronger and more in keeping with the hifil-stem force of the verb (tam'u = defile, make unclean). PNEUMA adds "that swarm on the ground" for the participle ha-romes; LOGOS uses "that crawls on the ground" (ramas = to crawl/swarm/creep). The consensus uses "that swarms on the ground" (PNEUMA) as more consistent with the chapter's sherets vocabulary.

---

### V. 45 — "who brought you up" — the specific exodus verb

**Hebrew:** asher he'eleti etkhem me-erets Mitsrayim (אֲשֶׁר הֶעֱלֵיתִי אֶתְכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם) — hifil of 'alah = "to bring up"

- **EMET:** "who brought you up from the land of Mitsrayim"
- **LOGOS:** "who brought you up out of the land of Egypt"
- **PNEUMA:** "who brought you up out of the land of Egypt"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "who brought you up out of the land of Egypt"
- **Rationale:** EMET preserves "Mitsrayim" (Hebrew form) — rejected per project convention (English name forms). All three agree on "brought you up" — correctly rendering he'eleti (hifil of 'alah, "to bring up/out/away"), which differs from the more common hotsi' ("to bring out") of the Decalogue formula. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on "out of"; the consensus follows.

---

### V. 45 — "You shall be holy, for I am holy" — wording

**Hebrew:** vihyitem qedoshim ki qadosh ani (וִהְיִיתֶם קְדֹשִׁים כִּי קָדוֹשׁ אָנִי)

- **EMET:** "and you shall be holy for I holy"
- **LOGOS:** "You shall therefore be holy, for I am holy."
- **PNEUMA:** "You shall be holy, because I am holy."
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "You shall be holy, for I am holy."
- **Rationale:** EMET drops the copula. LOGOS adds "therefore" which is not in the Hebrew of this specific verse (the "therefore" belongs to v. 44's vehiqqadishtem). PNEUMA's "because" is acceptable but "for" (LOGOS) better captures the tight formula-phrase rhythm. The consensus adopts PNEUMA's verse structure without "therefore" and LOGOS's "for."

---

### V. 46 — "law" vs "instruction" vs "Torah" for torah

**Hebrew:** zo't torat ha-behemah (זֹאת תּוֹרַת הַבְּהֵמָה) — "this is the torah of the animal"

- **EMET:** "This the Torah of the animal"
- **LOGOS:** "This is the law about the beast and the bird"
- **PNEUMA:** "This is the instruction for animals and birds"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "This is the instruction for animals and birds"
- **Rationale:** EMET preserves "Torah" (the Hebrew term itself), which in a summary context reads more as transliteration than translation. LOGOS's "law" imports the later canonical sense of "the Law." PNEUMA's "instruction" follows the SOPHIA ruling established in the Leviticus series for torah in summary formulas (capturing the pedagogical force of torah = teaching, showing the way). PNEUMA adopted.

---

### V. 46 — "beast" vs "animal" for behemah

**Hebrew:** behemah (בְּהֵמָה) in the summary formula

- **EMET:** "the animal"
- **LOGOS:** "the beast"
- **PNEUMA:** "animals"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "animals"
- **Rationale:** LOGOS's "beast" is archaic and misleading in contemporary English (where "beast" often connotes wildness or ferocity). "Animals" (PNEUMA/EMET broadly) is the preferred rendering. PNEUMA's plural "animals" is preferred for the summary formula, which covers the entire category.

---

### V. 47 — "to make a distinction" — le-havdil

**Hebrew:** le-havdil bein ha-tameh u-vein ha-tahor (לְהַבְדִּיל בֵּין הַטָּמֵא וּבֵין הַטָּהוֹר)

- **EMET:** "to distinguish between the unclean and between the clean"
- **LOGOS:** "to make a distinction between the unclean and the clean"
- **PNEUMA:** "to make a distinction between the unclean and the clean"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "to make a distinction between the unclean and the clean"
- **Rationale:** EMET's "to distinguish" is a defensible rendering of le-havdil (hifil of badal) but loses the connection to the priestly vocation formula of Lev 10:10 where le-havdil also appears. LOGOS and PNEUMA both use "to make a distinction," which echoes 10:10 consistently. Two-to-one; consensus follows.

---

### V. 47 — "living creature that may be eaten" vs "living creature that may not be eaten" — animals vs creatures

**Hebrew:** u-vein ha-chayyah ha-ne'ekelet u-vein ha-chayyah asher lo' te'akhel (וּבֵין הַחַיָּה הַנֶּאֱכֶלֶת וּבֵין הַחַיָּה אֲשֶׁר לֹא תֵאָכֵל)

- **EMET:** "between the living-thing that is to be eaten and between the living-thing that is not to be eaten"
- **LOGOS:** "between the living creature that may be eaten and the living creature that may not be eaten"
- **PNEUMA:** "between the animals that may be eaten and those that must not be eaten"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "between the living creature that may be eaten and the living creature that may not be eaten"
- **Rationale:** EMET's "living-thing" is too generic. PNEUMA's "animals" is narrower than ha-chayyah's scope in this summary (which covers all the creature categories of the chapter: land, water, air, insect). LOGOS's "living creature" is the broadest and most accurate. PNEUMA also drops the repetition of "living creature" (using "those" the second time), losing the parallel structure of the Hebrew. LOGOS adopted.
